title: ডিজিটাল নিরাপত্তা আইন, ২০১৮
description: The Digital Security Act 2018 establishes Bangladesh's primary legal framework for cybersecurity and digital security governance. It creates the Digital Security Agency and the Digital Security Council as the principal institutions responsible for cybersecurity policy and incident response, and establishes regulatory requirements applicable to information system operators and critical digital infrastructure. The Act was amended by the Cyber Security Act 2023 which replaced it.

provisions.title: Section 1. Short Title and Commencement
provisions.content: This Act may be called the Digital Security Act, 2018. It extends to the whole of Bangladesh. It came into force on such date as the Government may, by notification in the official Gazette, appoint. The Act establishes the legal framework for the protection of digital infrastructure, information systems, and electronic communications in Bangladesh. It creates the institutional architecture necessary for national cybersecurity governance and incident response, and establishes requirements applicable to critical digital infrastructure operators, government information systems, and providers of digital services.

provisions.title: Section 2. Definitions
provisions.content: In this Act, unless the context otherwise requires: 'Computer' means any electronic, magnetic, optical, electrochemical, or other high speed data processing device or system which performs logical, arithmetic, or memory functions by the manipulation of electronic, magnetic, or optical impulses, and includes all input, output, processing, storage, software, and communication facilities which are connected or related to such a device in a system or network; 'Computer system' means a device or combination of devices including input and output support devices and excluding calculators which are not programmable and capable of being used in conjunction with external files which contain computer programmes, electronic instructions, input data, and output data; 'Digital security' means the condition of protection of computer systems, networks, and digital data from theft, damage, or disruption; 'Critical information infrastructure' means those computer systems or networks, the incapacity or destruction of which would have a debilitating impact on national security, national economic security, national public health and safety, or any combination of those matters; 'Digital Security Agency' means the agency established under this Act.

provisions.title: Section 3. Digital Security Agency
provisions.content: There shall be established, for the purposes of this Act, an agency to be called the Digital Security Agency (DSA). The DSA shall be a body corporate with perpetual succession, having a common seal, with power to acquire, hold and dispose of property both movable and immovable, and to enter into contracts and shall by that name sue and be sued. The DSA shall be under the administrative control of the Ministry responsible for digital affairs. The Director General of the DSA shall be appointed by the Government. The principal functions of the DSA shall include: implementing national digital security policy; operating the national Computer Incident Response Team (CIRT); monitoring cybersecurity threats to critical information infrastructure; coordinating with international cybersecurity bodies; issuing digital security guidelines for government and critical infrastructure; and promoting digital security awareness.

provisions.title: Section 4. Digital Security Council
provisions.content: There shall be a Digital Security Council for the purpose of providing policy direction to the Government on digital security matters. The Digital Security Council shall be chaired by the Minister in charge of the Ministry responsible for digital affairs, and shall comprise the Ministers of Home, Finance, Foreign Affairs, Defence, and other relevant ministries, as well as representatives of relevant regulatory bodies. The Digital Security Council shall: formulate national digital security policy and strategy; coordinate digital security activities across government ministries and agencies; advise the Government on threats to critical information infrastructure; review and approve the national digital security plan; and ensure adequate resources for digital security activities. The Digital Security Council shall meet at least quarterly.

provisions.title: Section 5. National Digital Security Regulatory Framework
provisions.content: The Digital Security Agency shall establish and maintain a national digital security regulatory framework applicable to government information systems and critical information infrastructure. The framework shall specify: minimum security standards for government information systems; requirements for the protection of critical information infrastructure; incident reporting obligations; cybersecurity risk management requirements; and guidelines for the implementation of security controls. Government ministries, departments, and agencies shall comply with the framework and shall implement the required security measures within timeframes specified by the DSA. Critical infrastructure operators designated by the DSA shall be required to comply with applicable framework requirements and shall submit to periodic security assessments.

provisions.title: Section 6. Critical Information Infrastructure
provisions.content: The Government may, by notification in the official Gazette, declare any computer resource to be critical information infrastructure where the incapacity or destruction of such resource would have a debilitating impact on national security, national economic security, national public health or safety, or any combination of these. The owner or operator of a designated critical information infrastructure shall: implement security measures meeting standards specified by the DSA; report security incidents to the DSA within the specified timeframe; cooperate with the DSA in security assessments; and maintain business continuity plans. The DSA shall maintain a register of designated critical information infrastructure operators. The designation of critical information infrastructure shall be reviewed periodically and may be modified or revoked where circumstances change.

provisions.title: Section 7. National CIRT
provisions.content: The Digital Security Agency shall operate a national Computer Incident Response Team (CIRT) responsible for: receiving and analysing reports of cybersecurity incidents; coordinating the national response to significant cybersecurity incidents; providing technical assistance to government agencies and critical infrastructure operators; maintaining threat intelligence and sharing it with relevant stakeholders; issuing cybersecurity advisories and alerts; and participating in international CIRT networks. The CIRT shall operate around the clock and shall have the technical capability to detect, analyse, and respond to cybersecurity threats. Government agencies and critical infrastructure operators shall report significant cybersecurity incidents to the CIRT within the timeframes specified in applicable regulations.

provisions.title: Section 8. Regulatory Scope — Unauthorised Access
provisions.content: The regulatory framework established by this Act addresses the legal standards applicable to access to information systems and computer resources. Authorised access to an information system is defined as access by persons authorised by the system owner or operator, in accordance with applicable terms of use, contractual arrangements, or legal authority. Regulatory requirements applicable to information system operators include: implementing access controls proportionate to the sensitivity of the system; maintaining access logs enabling the review of access events; and implementing procedures for managing and reviewing access privileges. Government information systems and critical information infrastructure are subject to enhanced access control requirements specified in DSA guidelines. Operators are required to implement multi-factor authentication for privileged access to critical systems.

provisions.title: Section 9. Regulatory Scope — System Integrity and Malware
provisions.content: The Act addresses regulatory requirements for the protection of the integrity of information systems against unauthorised modification and malicious software. Information system operators are required to implement controls preventing: the introduction of malicious software into their systems; the unauthorised modification, damage, or deletion of data; and the disruption of system availability. Regulatory obligations applicable to software vendors and application distributors include: responsible disclosure of security vulnerabilities; timely release of security patches; and implementation of secure software development practices. The DSA may issue technical standards for malware protection applicable to government systems and critical information infrastructure. Operators are required to maintain malware detection and prevention capabilities and to update them regularly.

provisions.title: Section 10. Regulatory Scope — Electronic Identity and Authentication
provisions.content: The regulatory framework addresses requirements for the secure establishment and management of electronic identities and authentication credentials used to access information systems and digital services. Government digital services shall implement authentication mechanisms calibrated to the risk profile and sensitivity of each service. The national digital identity infrastructure shall provide a secure and reliable basis for authentication across government digital services. Private sector digital service providers are encouraged to implement strong authentication mechanisms and to participate in the national digital identity ecosystem. The DSA shall issue guidelines on authentication standards applicable to government systems and, as appropriate, to operators of critical information infrastructure. Electronic identity fraud shall be addressed through applicable law enforcement mechanisms.

provisions.title: Section 11. International Cooperation
provisions.content: The Government of Bangladesh shall cooperate with other countries and international organisations in matters relating to digital security, including through: information sharing on cyber threats and incidents; technical assistance and capacity building; mutual legal assistance in cybercrime investigations; and participation in international cybersecurity forums. The DSA shall represent Bangladesh in international cybersecurity bodies and shall maintain relationships with cybersecurity authorities in other countries. Bangladesh shall implement applicable international cybersecurity frameworks and shall align its national framework with relevant international standards. The DSA shall coordinate with the Ministry of Foreign Affairs on international cybersecurity cooperation matters, including the negotiation of bilateral cybersecurity cooperation agreements.

provisions.title: Section 12. Powers of Inspection and Investigation
provisions.content: The Digital Security Agency shall have powers to inspect information systems and investigate cybersecurity incidents where there are reasonable grounds to believe that a security incident has occurred or is imminent. Inspection powers shall be exercised by authorised DSA officers upon presentation of appropriate authority. System operators shall cooperate with DSA inspections and shall provide access to information systems, documentation, and personnel as required. Inspection findings shall be documented and, where violations of applicable requirements are identified, shall be communicated to the operator with requirements for remediation. The DSA shall maintain records of all inspections conducted and shall report on inspection activities in its annual report.

provisions.title: Section 13. Designation of Cyber Tribunal
provisions.content: The Government shall, by notification in the official Gazette, establish one or more Cyber Tribunals for trying offences under this Act. Each Cyber Tribunal shall consist of a Sessions Judge. The Cyber Tribunal shall exercise jurisdiction over offences specified in this Act. In trying offences under this Act, the Cyber Tribunal shall follow the procedure prescribed in the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898. The Cyber Tribunal may take cognisance of offences under this Act upon complaint by the DSA or upon a complaint by a person aggrieved. Decisions of the Cyber Tribunal may be appealed to the High Court Division.

provisions.title: Section 14. Cybersecurity Awareness and Capacity Building
provisions.content: The Digital Security Agency shall develop and implement national cybersecurity awareness programmes to promote safe and responsible use of digital technology among government employees, the private sector, and the general public. The DSA shall coordinate with the Ministry of Education, universities, and technical institutions to integrate cybersecurity into educational curricula at all levels. A national cybersecurity skills development programme shall be established to build the pipeline of cybersecurity professionals needed by the public and private sectors. The DSA shall maintain partnerships with international cybersecurity organisations and academic institutions to facilitate knowledge transfer and capacity building. Annual National Cybersecurity Awareness activities shall be organised to promote public understanding of cybersecurity risks and best practices.

provisions.title: Section 15. Transition to Cyber Security Act 2023
provisions.content: The Digital Security Act 2018 was substantially replaced by the Cyber Security Act 2023, which was enacted in response to concerns about the application of certain provisions of the DSA. The Cyber Security Act 2023 retained the institutional framework of the DSA including the Digital Security Agency, the Digital Security Council, and the national CIRT, while amending specific regulatory provisions. Cases pending under the DSA at the time of the enactment of the Cyber Security Act 2023 were transitioned in accordance with the transitional provisions of that Act. The Digital Security Agency continues to exercise its functions under the Cyber Security Act 2023 framework. This summary reflects the DSA 2018 as amended and as applicable during its operational period.

definitions.definition: Any electronic, magnetic, optical, or other high speed data processing device or system which performs logical, arithmetic, or memory functions.
Computer systems or networks whose incapacity or destruction would have a debilitating impact on national security, economic security, or public health and safety.
The government body established under this Act to implement digital security policy and operate national cybersecurity functions.
Computer Incident Response Team, responsible for receiving, analysing, and coordinating responses to cybersecurity incidents.
The condition of protection of computer systems, networks, and digital data from theft, damage, or disruption.
